coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots for 3D printed smartphone-assisted cascade visual detection of morin and aluminium ion, solid fluorescence ink
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Changhang
Wang, Ling
Xu, Kaixin
Xu, Sitong
Zhang, Ting
coredata.dc:description: With the growing emphasis on health management, individuals increasingly focus on monitoring drug molecular residues and harmful ions. Motivated by the concepts of the intelligent system and quantitative detection, we develop a smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for in-site quantitative detection of morin and aluminium ion (Al3+). Initially, citric acid and o-phenylenediamine are identified as precursors for N-CDs synthesis via the microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment. Subsequently, systematic studies reveal that the fluorescence of N-CDs is quenched upon the introduction of morin because of the inner filter effect (IFE). Conversely, the fluorescence recovery phenomenon occurs after the cascade addition of Al3+ since the morin binds with Al3+, resulting in the inhibition of IFE. Simultaneously, the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs enhances the cyan fluorescence emission of morin/Al3+. Based on the fluorescence quenching and recovery behavior, morin and Al3+ can be detected in the ranges of 0.070–50 μM and 0.124–40 μM with low detection limits of 0.070 μM and 0.124 μM, respectively. Furtherly, a smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device is designed for in-site quantitative detection of morin and Al3+. Moreover, based on the strong fluorescence and concentration-dependent emission behavior of N-CDs, the multi-color fluorescent ink is developed successfully. This work not only offers an excellent solution for simultaneous detection of morin and Al3+ but also shows numerous opportunities for solid lighting, information encryption and flexible optics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Cascade visual detection
Morin
Aluminium ion
3D printed technology
Fluorescence ink

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The utilization of smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device
Sensing principle about cascade detection of morin and Al3+
Cascade detection of morin and Al3+ using N-CDs
3D printed smartphone-assisted cascade visual detection of morin and Al3+
A colorful fluorescence N-CDs ink for patterning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by projects from Talent-introduction Training Special Project of NBPT (No. NZ23RC03), National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

This work was supported by projects from Scientific Research Fund of Zhejiang Provincial Education Department (Y202147342), National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084 ) and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University . The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and high-resolution images of N-CDs (a). Size distribution (b) and FT-IR spectrum (c) of N-CDs. High-resolution XPS C 1 s (d), N 1 s (e) and O 1 s (f) spectra of N-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of N-CDs (Inset shows the pictures of N-CDs upon the irradiation of visible light and 365 nm excitation Source). (b) Emission spectra of N-CDs under the excitation wavelength from 350 to 420 nm. (c) Tauc-plot for bandgap calculation in solutions with different concentrations of N-CDs. The fluorescence stability experiments involved various NaCl concentration (d), store time (e) and irradiation time (f).

The 3D fluorescence spectra of N-CDs (a), with morin (b) and with morin+Al3+ (c). (d) The absorption spectra of various combinations of N-CDs, morin and Al3+, and their linear additions. (e) The fluorescence lifetimes of N-CDs, N-CDs/morin and N-CDs/morin+Al3+. (f) Mechanism illustration of the cascade detection for morin and Al3+.

(a) Emission spectra of N-CDs with morin of various concentrations from 0 to 130 μM. (b) The linear relationship about the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and morin concentration (I440 nm, 0 is the intensity at 440 nm of the N-CDs solution without quenchers). (c) The fitting results of the linear relationship between N-CDs fluorescence and the morin concentration. (d) Emission spectra of N-CDs/morin with Al3+ of various concentrations from 0 to 120 μM. (e) The linear relationship about the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs/morin and Al3+ concentration. (f) The fitting results of the linear relationship between N-CDs/morin fluorescence and Al3+ concentration.

(a) The visual sensing route of the designed device. (b) The linear relationship and the fitting results of the linear relationship for morin detection. (c) The linear relationship and the fitting results of the linear relationship for Al3+ detection. (d) The practical sample analysis using the device.

(a) N-CDs inks (5 mg/mL, 50 mg/mL, 120 mg/mL and 180 mg/mL). Images of imprinted dragon patterns and 2D codes (b) and hand-written sentences (c) using the N-CD-based fluorescence ink on a rice paper under UV light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the preparation procedure of N-CDs (a), the cascade detection of morin and Al3+ (b) and multi-color fluorescence ink applications (c).

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots for 3D printed smartphone-assisted cascade visual detection of morin and aluminium ion, solid fluorescence ink

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ningbo Polytechnic, Ningbo, 315800, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Ningbo Polytechnic, Ningbo, 315800, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the growing emphasis on health management, individuals increasingly focus on monitoring drug molecular residues and harmful ions. Motivated by the concepts of the intelligent system and quantitative detection, we develop a smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for in-site quantitative detection of morin and aluminium ion (Al3+). Initially, citric acid and o-phenylenediamine are identified as precursors for N-CDs synthesis via the microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment. Subsequently, systematic studies reveal that the fluorescence of N-CDs is quenched upon the introduction of morin because of the inner filter effect (IFE). Conversely, the fluorescence recovery phenomenon occurs after the cascade addition of Al3+ since the morin binds with Al3+, resulting in the inhibition of IFE. Simultaneously, the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs enhances the cyan fluorescence emission of morin/Al3+. Based on the fluorescence quenching and recovery behavior, morin and Al3+ can be detected in the ranges of 0.070–50 μM and 0.124–40 μM with low detection limits of 0.070 μM and 0.124 μM, respectively. Furtherly, a smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device is designed for in-site quantitative detection of morin and Al3+. Moreover, based on the strong fluorescence and concentration-dependent emission behavior of N-CDs, the multi-color fluorescent ink is developed successfully. This work not only offers an excellent solution for simultaneous detection of morin and Al3+ but also shows numerous opportunities for solid lighting, information encryption and flexible optics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Morin, a natural dye, can serve as a mordant dye for producing yellow or green light olive via various mordants [1]. Additionally, morin is a natural active substance with antioxidant and hypoglycemic effects and can be used to treat viral infection, stomach disease, chronic inflammation and coronary heart disease [2]. Given its wide-ranging market potential and application scenarios, monitoring morin, whether inside or outside the body, becomes crucial for biology and environment safety [3]. Apart from morin, the presence of excessive metal ions can also pose unforeseen hazards. Notably, aluminum is a prevalent metal in various materials [4]. However, due to unavoidable circumstances, some aluminum ions (Al3+) are released into water environment, leading to inadvertent excess intake of Al3+. This poses a threat to the bones, brain and nervous system, and even contributes to the disease such as Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s and breast cancer [5]. Consequently, based on numerous hazard reports and systematic studies, the World Health Organization (WHO) stipulated the maximum allowable level of Al3+ in drinking water, approximately 7.14 μM [6]. Hence, it is extremely urgent to monitor the morin and Al3+ content in common environment.

Inspired by portable, in-site and quantitative sensing technologies, numerous researches industriously seek to cross and fuse with this domain. For instance, Tang et al. designed a smartphone-integrated optical sensing platform combined with biomass derived CDs for real-time detection of Ag+ [18]. Guo et al. structured a visual sensing device for bimodal excitation-based ratiometric detection of fluoride ion with the support of mobile phones [19]. Leveraging the RGB (Red, Green and Blue) analysis function of smartphone, the combination of fluorescence sensor and analyte shows the current lightness, which can be captured by the smartphone camera and further quantified to reflect the analyte concentration. In addition, with the advancement of additive manufacturing technology, three-dimensional (3D) printing technology emerges as a potential substitution for traditional manufacturing methods because of its diverse raw materials, precise structural design and the simplified preparation process [20]. Significantly, when combining the smartphone-assisted sensing technology with 3D printing technology, not only the operational processes are streamlined, but the cost of detection is also minimized [21,22]. Therefore, to enhance the feasibility of visual sensing and reduce the cost of the sensing technology, an excellent choice is to make a fusion of 3D printing technology, smartphone and CDs-based fluorescence materials.

In this work, we efficiently synthesize blue carbon dots through microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment. Systematic studies demonstrate that the absorption spectrum of morin overlaps well with the excitation spectrum of N-CDs, without the formation of new compounds. Based on the inner filter effect (IFE), the N-CDs fluorescence quenches upon the addition of morin. A well-established linear relationship correlates the fluorescence quenching degrees with the morin concentration in the range from 0.070 to 50 μM, boasting a low detection limit of 0.070 μM. Upon the cascade addition of Al3+, the fluorescence recovery occurs because of the robust chelation between morin and Al3+, resulting from the inhibition of IFE. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of N-CDs enhances the cyan fluorescence of morin/Al3+ chelate, showing a red-shift phenomenon visually. Similarly, the Al3+ detection can be realized within the range of 0.124 to 40 μM with a detection limit of 0.124 μM. Notably, motivated by the smartphone and portable sensing technologies, a smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device is designed for in-site quantitative detection of morin and Al3+ with good practicability. Furthermore, based on the strong fluorescence and concentration-dependent emission behavior of N-CDs, the multi-color fluorescent ink is realized. In summary, the present work not merely provides a simple and efficient “One Stone Two Birds” strategy for cascade detection of morin and Al3+ but also achieves the applications of high quantum yield N-CDs in the fields of fluorescent ink.

O-phenylenediamine (OPD), citric acid (CA) and other chemicals were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water used throughout all experiments was sourced from the Millipore system. All chemical materials were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification.

The morphologies of N-CDs were characterized by JEM 2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 2100F). The surface structures and elemental constituents of N-CDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Bruker, WQF-410), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ULVAC-PHI), and X-ray Diffraction (XRD, PANalytical X’Pert PRO diffractometer). The optical features of N-CDs and the cascade sensing system were measured by UV–vis absorption (UV-5800 spectrophotometer), photoluminescence (PL) and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra (PerkinElmer Instruments). In addition, the measurement of fluorescence lifetime was implemented to investigate the sensing mechanism on a Horiba FL3-111 spectrometer. Moreover, all photos were taken by a smartphone with the HUAWEI P40 model.

N-CDs were obtained via a microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. Initially, 2 mmol of OPD and 3 mmol of CA were mixed in 50 mL ultrapure water with ultrasonic dispersion for 20 min. Subsequently, the mixture was poured in the microwave digestion tank with a treatment of microwave power of 200 W. After reaction of 2 h and being cooled down to the room temperature, the original N-CDs solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Furtherly, the treated N-CDs solution was freeze-dried to gain the powder for the follow-up experiments.

Firstly, 1 mL of morin solution at different concentrations and 2 mL of N-CDs solution were completely reacted in a cuvette at room temperature for 5 min. Then, the fluorescence excitation spectra of the mixture were scanned by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (λem = 440 nm). Besides, the morin concentration was fixed at 30 μM for the cascade detection of Al3+. Similarly, 1 mL of Al3+ solution at different concentrations and 2 mL of N-CDs/morin solution were mixed and incubated for 5 min. Finally, the excitation spectra were obtained to explore the relationship between the intensity ratio value and Al3+ concentration (I485 nm/I440 nm , I485 nm and I440 nm are the signal intensities at 485 nm and 440 nm, respectively). The detection experiments were conducted three times to calculate the average values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The utilization of smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device
ce:section.ce:para: Motivated by the function of in-site detection and smartphones, we designed a smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device containing a 3D printed sample loader, a sensing strip and an ultraviolet irradiation source. So as to ensure the validity of detection results, two lines (test line (T) and control line (C)) were designed. The detection results could be accepted only if the C generates the chromogenic reaction, meaning that the sample had flowed through the T and C, and fully contacted with the sensor. Furtherly, upon the irradiation of ultraviolet source, the fluorescence photos from T were captured by the HUAWEI P40. Similarly, Al3+ was determined by the same step except for exchanging the sensing strip loading the N-CDs/morin system. Finally, the fluorescence pictures were analyzed by the RGB (Red, Green and Blue) analysis software. Through software processing, the fluorescence signals of respective samples were evaluated to reflect the analyte content.

The morphology and elementary composition are characterized by TEM, XRD, FT-IR and XPS technologies. Initial observations of the TEM image of N-CDs (Fig. 1
(a)) indicate that N-CDs are dispersed well with a quasi-spherical feature. The high-resolution TEM image displays a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm, matching the (1120) facet of graphite [23]. Simultaneously, statistical analysis of the TEM image shows an average size of 3.5 nm, ranging from 1 to 7 nm (Fig. 1 (b)). Besides, the XRD pattern reveals the peak at 2θ = 21° ( Fig. S1 ), confirming the graphitic structure of N-CDs [24].

In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1 (c)), the predominant characteristic band of –OH/–NH2 hydrophilic groups can be observed from 3210 to 3412 cm−1, contributing to the good solubility in solvents [25,26]. The characteristic peaks at 1703 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibrations of C=C groups. Additionally, the stretching vibrations of C-O and C=O result in the occurrence of characteristic peaks at 1102 cm−1 and 1582 cm−1, respectively. Peaks at 1258 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibrations of C-N [27]. These measurement results indicate that the N-CDs surface possesses abundant nitrogen-related and oxygen-related functional groups. Additionally, the full XPS spectrum ( Fig. S2 ) reveals that the N-CDs contain three main elements, including C (284.5 eV, 73.0%), N (399.8 eV, 14.1%) and O (531.9 eV, 12.9%). Meanwhile, the respective high-resolution XPS spectra are analyzed. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 1 (d)) shows that three peaks at 284.9 eV, 285.7 eV and 288.2 eV are assigned to the C=C, C-N and C-O/C=O, respectively [27,28]. In the Fig. 1 (e), the N 1 s spectrum of N-CDs is decomposed into two signals of the C-N (399.3 eV) and N-H (401.1 eV) [29]. The fitting peaks of O 1 s at 531.6 eV and 532.3 eV result from the C=O and C-O, respectively [30]. The XPS results are nearly consistent with the FT-IR characterizations, confirming the presence of abundant nitrogen-related and oxygen-related groups on the surface of N-CDs.

As depicted in Fig. 2
(a), the as-prepared N-CDs emit a bright blue fluorescence upon the excitation of ultraviolet light in water. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-CDs presents a hump peak located at 247 nm, which correspond to the π-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains [31]. Whereas, two absorption peaks at 275 nm and 365 nm are ascribed to the n-π* transition of C=O and C-N [32,33]. With the optimal excitation wavelength of 375 nm, N-CDs show a maximum emission wavelength of 440 nm. Subsequently, under excitation wavelength ranging from 350 to 420 nm, a series of emission spectra are obtained, indicating the excitation-dependent behavior of N-CDs (Fig. 2 (b)). Besides, the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs solution at various concentrations are measured. The normalized emission spectra reveal a red shift phenomenon from 440 to 550 nm with the increase of concentration ( Fig. S3 ). Concomitantly, the absorption spectra of N-CDs at different concentrations are transformed to obtain the Tauc plots of (αhυ)2 verses hυ, indicating gradual reduction in the energy gap (Fig. 2 (c)). According to previous reports, with the increase of N-CDs concentration, aggregation occurs to counteract instability resulting from the concentration variation [34]. Meanwhile, the absorption of N-CDs tends to red-shift. Owing to the effect of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or re-absorption, the short-wavelength emission part of N-CDs overlaps with the absorption band, resulting in weak short-wavelength emission but relatively insusceptible long-wavelength emission ( Fig. S4 ) [35,36]. Therefore, as the N-CDs concentration increases, a red shift occurs in the fluorescence of N-CDs. In addition, the fluorescence stabilities of N-CDs are explored under various NaCl concentrations, storage time and irradiation time. Notably, N-CDs show insensitivity to NaCl concentration variation from 0 to 22.5 mg/mL (Fig. 2 (d)). Additionally, after 15 days and 20 h of irradiation, N-CDs fluorescence experiences a negligible reduction (Fig. 2 (e-f)). Therefore, according the abovementioned characterization, N-CDs possess a favorable structure and fluorescence properties that enables them to achieve excellent performance in optical sensors and display techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing principle about cascade detection of morin and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para:
For the purpose of exploring the sensing principle about cascade detection of morin and Al3+ using N-CDs, systematic studies are performed. Fig. 3
(a) shows the 3D fluorescence spectra of N-CDs, providing the detailed excitation and emission positions. Upon the addition of morin, the absorption spectrum of morin overlaps well with the excitation spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 3 (b)). Subsequently, after the cascade addition of Al3+, the absorption spectrum of morin+Al3+ shows a red shift behavior, while the fluorescence of N-CDs recovers (Fig. 3 (c)). Based on these observations, we preliminarily infer that IFE is the dominant mechanism governing fluorescence quenching and recovery since it can be triggered by the simple spectral overlap [21]. To validate this hypothesis, systematic experiments are performed. As presented in Fig. 3 (d), the absorption spectra of various combinations of N-CDs, morin and Al3+ are measured. The results reveal that the addition of Al3+ leads to the red shift in the morin absorption due to the strong chelation between Al3+ and morin. However, compared the spectra of N-CDs after adding the morin, Al3+ and morin+Al3+ with their respective physical sum, no new peak is evident, indicating the absence of complex formation [37]. Moreover, the average fluorescence lifetimes are 8.48 ns, 8.63 ns and 8.73 ns for N-CDs, N-CDs/morin and N-CDs/morin+Al3+, respectively. Obviously, these lifetimes fluctuate within an ignorable range (Fig. 3 (e)), demonstrating the presence of efficient IFE, whether morin to N-CDs or morin/Al3+ to N-CDs [21]. Furthermore, Al3+ chelates with morin to reduce the degree of overlap between N-CDs excitation and morin absorption, thereby inhibiting the quenching effect from IFE. Interestingly, at the same time as the fluorescence of N-CDs (440 nm) recovering, the fluorescence state of morin+Al3+ chelate (485 nm) is excited and further enhanced due to the absorption of partial excitation light of N-CDs absorbed by the chelate. Therefore, morin can quench the N-CDs fluorescence whereas the cascade addition of Al3+ slightly restores the fluorescence of N-CDs and results in the cyan morin+Al3+ chelate fluorescence enhancement. The schematic diagram illustrating the cascade effect for the fluorescence quenching and recovery is displayed in Fig. 3 (f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cascade detection of morin and Al3+ using N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Utilizing these interesting effects, the cascade detection of morin and Al3+ is realized by N-CDs. Before the experiments, several parameters are adjusted to achieve optimal detection performance, including the N-CDs content, morin concentration, pH and incubation time. Considering the maximization of detection performance, the effect of pH on the cascade detection of morin and Al3+ is investigated. As shown in Fig. S5 (a), the fluorescence of N-CDs exhibits a pH inertness with the range of 4–9 but quenches dramatically under extremely acidic and alkaline conditions. Previous studies reported that the change of molecular structure or the electronic structure from CDs possibly leads to the fluorescence quenching since there is a strong relationship between the structural features and fluorescence properties of CDs [38]. For the detection of morin, it is found that the quenching efficiency is optimal at pH = 8 (Fig. S5 (b)). However, upon the cascade detection of Al3+, the intensity ratio (I485 nm /I440 nm ) tends to reach optimum values around pH = 7 and 8 (Fig. S5 (c)). Thus, to simplify environmental pH regulation, pH = 8 is selected as the detection environment pH. Furthermore, with the N-CDs and Al3+ concentrations set at 0.5 mg·mL−1 and 50 μM, the morin content for Al3+ detection is studied. The results reveal that the I485 nm/I440 nm reaches to a peak value after the addition of 30 μM of morin ( Fig. S6 ). Consequently, 30 μM of morin is determined for Al3+ detection. Afterwards, the incubation time is chosen to be 3 min based on the experiment result in Fig. S7 . Hence, the conditions for the cascade detection of morin and Al3+ are optimized as pH = 8, 30 μM morin and 3 min incubation time.

Under optional conditions, the cascade detection of morin and Al3+ is performed. Morin with different concentrations is added to the N-CDs solution, and the fluorescence spectra are recorded in Fig. 4
(a), representing the fluorescence quenching with the morin concentration increasing (Fig. 4 (b)). Fig. 4 (c) demonstrates an excellent linear relationship within the range from 0.070 to 50 μM with the LOD calculated as 0.070 μM according to the equation (LOD = 3σ/s, where σ and s denote the standard deviation of the corrected blank signals and the slope of the calibration curve, respectively). Similarly, the detection performance of Al3+ is determined using the same procedures. Al3+ can be detected in the range of 0.124–40 μM with the LOD of 0.124 μM (Fig. 4 (d-f)). Besides, to assess the selectivity of the sensing system, various interference substance, such as NH4 +, Na+, Mg2+, Al3+, K+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cl-, Br-, I-, CO3 2–, NO3 –, HPO4 2-, SO4 2-, Sudan I, Quinoline yellow, Indigo, Amaranth, D-fructose, Glucose, Sucrose, L-glutamic acid, L-glycine, L-tyrosine and L-threonine, are introduced into the N-CDs solution and the N-CDs/morin system. It is worth noting that the N-CDs fluorescence remains insensitive to the addition of most of these interference substances unless the morin is present ( Fig. S8 ). In the N-CDs/morin system, only Al3+ enables fluorescence recovery ( Fig. S9 ). Therefore, through the systematic detection and analysis, we conclude that N-CDs enable the cascade detection of morin and Al3+ with wide detection ranges and low LODs. Significantly, in comparison with other detection technologies and CDs-based sensors, our work presents comparable or even superior detection performance ( Table S1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3D printed smartphone-assisted cascade visual detection of morin and Al3+
ce:section.ce:para:
Enlightened by the portable sensing device, we design a 3D printed smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device, which possesses the characteristics, such as low-cost, portability, practicability and accuracy. Initially, the geometrical structures of the device are engineered using the computer-aided design (CAD) (Fig. 5 (a)). Then, the 3D printer is employed to structure the device model using the polylactic acid (PLA) material. This device contains several parts, including the smartphone holder, the sample cells and the built-in UV lamp. After putting the pre-treated samples in the cells and activating the UV lamp, leave to set for the sample flowing through T and C. Once the chromogenic reaction generates, the fluorescence images of T can be captured using the smartphone camera. By converting the signals of images into the RGB values, the respective concentrations of morin and Al3+ are fitted with the B and G+B values [39]. Therefore, good linear relationships are established within ranges of 0.153–50 μM and 0.264–60 μM with LODs of 0.153 μM and 0.264 μM for morin and Al3+ (Fig. 5
(b-c)). In addition, three practical samples (saliva, river water and canned water were treated by steps in Supporting information ) are selected to evaluate the practicability of this device (Fig. 5 (d)). The results are listed in Table S2 with satisfactory recoveries of 96.1–114.2%, which expresses great potentials of this 3D printed smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device in the daily environmental monitoring and health determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A colorful fluorescence N-CDs ink for patterning
ce:section.ce:para:
Apart from the superior cascade detection performance for morin and Al3+, N-CDs also show the excellent dispersibility, stability and concentration-dependent fluorescence properties (Fig. 6
(a)). As a result, they are employed in the fabrication of fluorescence inks. For patterning and writing applications, a series of N-CDs solutions (5 mg/mL, 50 mg/mL, 120 mg/mL and 180 mg/mL) are prepared, displaying respective fluorescence color by adjusting the N-CDs content in solution. Subsequently, the as-prepared N-CDs solution at various concentrations is immitted into seals engraved with the logo of dragon and 2D code. The idiographic printing process is illustrated in Fig. S10 . Consequently, multi-color logo of dragon and 2D code are obtained as shown in Fig. 6 (b). Moreover, the N-CDs solution is loaded into an empty marker pen to write words on the rice paper. Notably, the writing on the rice paper is nearly unobservable in daylight. However, under UV light, the writing exhibits a bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 6 (c)). Hence, the fluorescence N-CDs ink reveals a great potential in the applications of patterning and writing.

In summary, N-CDs are rapidly synthesized using citric acid and o-phenylenediamine via ultraviolet radiation treatment, exhibiting superior stability and fluorescence properties. Interestingly, N-CDs become possible to detect morin based on the IFE. Namely, the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs can overlap well with the morin absorption. Upon the cascade detection of Al3+, morin chelates with Al3+, consequently impacting the morin absorption to inhibit the efficiency of IFE. Interestingly, the N-CDs fluorescence at 440 nm recovers slightly while the cyan fluorescence at 485 nm enhances dramatically. As a result, the cascade detection of morin and Al3+ can be realized within wide detection ranges of 0.070–50 μM and 0.124–40 μM with low LODs of 0.070 μM and 0.124 μM, respectively. Furtherly, encouraged by the 3D printed technology and portable device, a 3D printed smartphone-assisted cascade visual sensing device is successfully designed and applied in the practical sample analysis with satisfactory recoveries. Moreover, the bright fluorescence and concentration-dependent characteristic of N-CDs are exploited as a multi-color fluorescence ink for patterning and writing. This work achieves high-efficiency cascade detection of morin and Al3+ and the development of multi-color fluorescence ink, providing numerous opportunities for environmental monitoring, health determination and fluorescent patterning and writing.

Changhang Ma: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Ling Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Kaixin Xu: Visualization, Supervision, Formal analysis. Sitong Xu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Ting Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by projects from Talent-introduction Training Special Project of NBPT (No. NZ23RC03), National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant (No. 61604084) and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University. The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Electronic Supplementary Information (ESI) available: [details of any supplementary information available should be included here]. Figures (Figures S1-S10). Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115711.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inhibitory effects of plant-derived flavonoids and phenolic acids on malonaldehyde formation from ethyl arachidonate

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Lee, T. Shibamoto, G.R. Takeoka, S. Lee, J. Kim, B. Park, Inhibitory effects of plant-derived flavonoids and phenolic acids on malonaldehyde formation from ethyl arachidonate, J. Agric. Food Chem. 51 (2003) 7203-7207.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Morin hydrate augments phagocytosis mechanism and inhibits LPS induced autophagic signaling in murine macrophage

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The simultaneous separation and determination of six flavonoids and troxerutin in rat urine and chicken plasma by reversed-phase high-performance liquid chromatography with ultraviolet-visible detection
